intro: The Potter's Field was the piece of ground purchased with the thirty pieces of silver returned by Judas Iscariot to the chief priests after his betrayal of Jesus (Matt. 27:7). The priests, unable to put blood money into the temple treasury, used it to buy a field for burying foreigners. Matthew identifies this as a fulfillment of Jeremiah's prophecy (Matt. 27:9–10; cf. Jer. 32:6–9; Zech. 11:12–13). The field was afterward known as Hakeldama — "Field of Blood" (Acts 1:19). Luke's account in Acts 1:18–19 says Judas himself acquired the field with the wages of his wickedness and that he fell there. Tradition places Hakeldama in the valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem.
